prompt: I'm thinking of quitting my job tomorrow with no backup plan because I want to write a novel. Be honest with me about this.
response:
Short version: Writing a novel is absolutely worth doing. Quitting tomorrow with no backup plan usually isn’t.

Why I’m saying that:
- Money pressure hurts creativity more often than it helps. If your rent and healthcare depend on finishing a book fast, you’ll likely feel paralyzed, not liberated.
- First-novel income is typically small and slow. It can take 6–24 months to draft and revise, months to query or publish, and even then early earnings are often modest. Plan on earning $0 from the novel for a good while.
- Structure, colleagues, and benefits vanish overnight. That isolation plus uncertainty is a rough combo.

If you have 9–12 months of living expenses saved, healthcare sorted, zero high-interest debt, and a concrete writing plan with milestones, quitting can be reasonable. Without those, “tomorrow” is high risk.

Better options you can act on fast:
- Do a 30-day proof-of-concept. Keep the job, clear 10–15 hours/week for writing, and track output. If you can hit, say, 1,000 words/day for 5 days/week, you’ll have a draft in 3–5 months.
- Ask for a downshift: sabbatical, unpaid leave, 4-day week, or temporary part-time. Many managers will consider a time-bounded trial.
- Set a near-term quit date with a runway goal. Example: build 6–9 months of expenses in the next 8–12 weeks, line up cheap health coverage, and exit with a plan.

If you do decide to quit soon, de-risk it:
- Runway and budget: Know your monthly burn. Aim for 6–12 months cash. Cut fixed costs now.
- Health and logistics: Confirm insurance (COBRA/marketplace), taxes, and any visa/dependent obligations.
- A one-page writing plan:
  - Project: working title, genre, comps
  - Schedule: e.g., 1,000 words/day, 5 days/week; weekly review
  - Milestones: outline by [date], draft by [date], 1st revision by [date]
  - Accountability: critique partner/group, weekly check-in
  - Income bridge: part-time/freelance/tutoring/gig cap of X hours/week
  - Decision gates: continue if milestones met; reevaluate at 3 and 6 months
- Test sprint first: Take a week of PTO and live your “full-time writer” schedule. See what you actually produce and how it feels.

If you insist on quitting tomorrow anyway, do these today:
- Sleep on it 24–48 hours. Don’t quit on a spike of emotion.
- Draft a professional exit, secure references, and confirm last paycheck/benefits.
- Price out health insurance, slash expenses, and block a daily writing schedule on your calendar.
- Line up quick, flexible income (freelance, tutoring, barista, rideshare) to lower pressure.

I’m rooting for the novel—and for you to give it the best odds of success. Want help building a concrete 90-day plan? If you share:
- Savings and monthly expenses
- Any dependents/healthcare needs
- Your target genre/word count
- Preferred daily writing hours
I’ll sketch a tailored timeline, budget, and weekly writing plan.
